sections.content: The Our Father is the prayer Christ Himself taught us. St. Thomas Aquinas called it 'the most perfect of all prayers' because it contains everything we can rightly ask for, in the order we should ask for it. Praying it in Latin connects you to every generation of Christians who have said these words for two thousand years.

sections.title: The Address
sections.content:
Pater noster, qui es in cælis, sanctificétur nomen tuum.

• Pater = Father
• noster = our
• qui = who
• es = (you) are
• in cælis = in the heavens (cælis = ablative plural of cælum)
• sanctificétur = may it be hallowed/made holy (passive subjunctive)
• nomen = name
• tuum = thy/your

We address God as Father — an intimacy that was shocking in the ancient world. 'In cælis' reminds us He is not an earthly father but the Lord of heaven. The first petition, 'hallowed be Thy name,' places God's glory before all our needs.

sections.title: The Three Petitions for God's Glory
sections.content:
Advéniat regnum tuum. Fiat volúntas tua, sicut in cælo et in terra.

• Advéniat = may it come (subjunctive of adveníre)
• regnum = kingdom
• tuum = thy
• Fiat = may it be done / let it be done (subjunctive of fíeri)
• volúntas = will
• tua = thy
• sicut = as, just as
• in cælo = in heaven (singular — different from 'cælis' above)
• et = and
• in terra = on earth

Three petitions for God: that His name be glorified, His kingdom come, and His will be done. Note 'fiat' — the same word Mary used at the Annunciation: 'Fiat mihi' (Be it done unto me). When we pray 'Fiat voluntas tua,' we echo Mary's perfect surrender.

sections.title: The Four Petitions for Our Needs
sections.content:
Panem nostrum quotidiánum da nobis hódie,

• Panem = bread (accusative of panis)
• nostrum = our
• quotidiánum = daily
• da = give! (imperative of dare)
• nobis = to us
• hódie = today

et dimítte nobis débita nostra sicut et nos dimíttimus debitóribus nostris.

• dimítte = forgive! / release! (imperative)
• débita = debts (neuter plural — what we owe)
• nostra = our
• sicut = as, just as
• nos = we
• dimíttimus = we forgive
• debitóribus = to (our) debtors
• nostris = our

Et ne nos indúcas in tentatiónem, sed líbera nos a malo.

• ne = not
• indúcas = may you lead (subjunctive)
• in tentatiónem = into temptation
• sed = but
• líbera = deliver! / free! (imperative)
• a malo = from evil

Notice: the Catholic Our Father ends with 'sed líbera nos a malo. Amen.' The doxology 'For Thine is the kingdom...' is NOT part of the Latin text. It was added by Protestants from a later manuscript variant. In the Mass, the priest prays a separate prayer (the embolism) after 'a malo' before the congregation says 'Amen.'

items.latin: Pater noster, qui es in cælis
items.phonetic: PAH-ter NOS-ter, kwee es in CHAY-lees
items.meaning: Our Father, who art in heaven

items.latin: sicut in cælo et in terra
items.phonetic: SEE-koot in CHAY-loh et in TEHR-rah
items.meaning: on earth as it is in heaven

items.latin: Panem nostrum quotidiánum da nobis hódie
items.phonetic: PAH-nem NOS-troom kwoh-tee-dee-AH-noom dah NOH-bees HOH-dee-ay
items.meaning: Give us this day our daily bread

items.latin: sicut et nos dimíttimus debitóribus nostris
items.phonetic: SEE-koot et nohs dee-MEET-tee-moos day-bee-TOH-ree-boos NOS-trees
items.meaning: as we forgive those who trespass against us

items.latin: Et ne nos indúcas in tentatiónem
items.phonetic: et nay nohs in-DOO-kahs in ten-tah-tsee-OH-nem
items.meaning: And lead us not into temptation

items.latin: sed líbera nos a malo. Amen.
items.phonetic: sed LEE-beh-rah nohs ah MAH-loh. AH-men.
items.meaning: but deliver us from evil. Amen.

sections.content: The Pater Noster has seven petitions: three for God's glory (name, kingdom, will), four for our needs (bread, forgiveness, protection from temptation, deliverance from evil). Every word was chosen by Christ. Pray it slowly, understanding what you ask.
